introduction.p: Point your browser to the Desmos worksheet at http://gvsu.edu/s/0zv. In what follows, we explore the behavior of power functions y = x^n where n \le -1.

statement.p: Press the play button next to the slider labeled n. Watch two loops of the animation and then discuss the trends that you observe. Write a careful sentence each for at least two different trends.

solution.p: Two trends: (1) For even-n exponents (like x^{-2}, x^{-4}, \ldots), the graph is symmetric about the y-axis and stays positive; for odd exponents (like x^{-1}, x^{-3}, \ldots), the graph is negative for x \lt 0 and positive for x \gt 0. (2) As the exponent becomes more negative, the graph becomes more sharply L-shaped near x = 0.

statement.p: Click the icons next to each of the following 8 functions so that you can see all of y = x^{-1}, y = x^{-2}, \ldots, y = x^{-8} graphed at once. On the interval 1 \lt x, how do the functions x^a and x^b compare if a \lt b? (Be careful with negative numbers here: e.g., -3 \lt -2.)

solution.p: On x \gt 1, x^a \lt x^b when a \lt b (with both negative). For example, x^{-3} \lt x^{-2} for x \gt 1, since dividing by a higher power gives a smaller result.

statement.p: How do your answers change on the interval 0 \lt x \lt 1?

solution.p: On 0 \lt x \lt 1, the situation reverses: x^a \gt x^b when a \lt b (i.e., more negative exponents give larger values on (0,1) since dividing by a small number raised to a high power gives a large result).

statement.p: Uncheck the icons on each of the 8 functions to hide their graphs. Click the settings icon to change the domain settings for the axes, and change them to -10 \le x \le 10 and -10,000 \le y \le 10,000. Play the animation through twice and then discuss the trends that you observe. Write a careful sentence each for at least two different trends.

solution.p: Two trends with the wider window: (1) The graphs with even exponents are symmetric about the y-axis; odd-exponent graphs are antisymmetric. (2) As the exponent becomes more negative, the function rises more steeply near x = 0 (steeper asymptotic behavior).

statement.p: Explain why \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x^n} = 0 for any choice of n = 1, 2, \ldots.

solution.p: As x \to \infty, x^n \to \infty for any positive integer n, so \frac{1}{x^n} = x^{-n} \to 0. Dividing 1 by an ever-larger number drives the result to 0.
